items.correct: Unemployment caused by a mismatch between workers' skills/location and available jobs — often due to long-term economic change like deindustrialisation.
items.misconception: Structural unemployment is unemployment caused by a recession.
items.random_wrong: Structural unemployment is when people are too lazy to work.
items.why: Recession-driven unemployment is CYCLICAL. Structural is about long-term mismatches — coal miners can't easily become app developers. Students collapse all unemployment into 'lack of demand'.

items.q: What is the difference between fixed and variable costs?
items.correct: Fixed costs don't change with output (e.g. rent, salaries); variable costs change directly with output (e.g. raw materials, hourly wages).
items.misconception: Fixed costs never change at all, ever.
items.random_wrong: Fixed costs are paid once and variable costs are paid daily.
items.why: Fixed costs are fixed WITH RESPECT TO OUTPUT — they can change for other reasons (rent goes up). The distinction is whether producing more or less changes the cost.

items.q: What is the difference between real GDP, nominal GDP and GNI?
items.correct: Nominal GDP uses current prices; real GDP adjusts for inflation; GNI (Gross National Income) is GDP plus net income from abroad (similar to old GNP). Each answers a different question about economic activity.
items.misconception: Real GDP, nominal GDP and GNI are interchangeable terms.
items.random_wrong: Real GDP is GDP only from physical goods; nominal GDP includes services.
items.why: Each measure has a precise role. Comparing growth over time needs real GDP; comparing across years uses nominal cautiously; GNI captures returns from foreign holdings. Students treat them as synonyms and misuse data.

items.q: Who introduced the concept of Nash equilibrium?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Nash. “Smith” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: At a Nash equilibrium each player:
items.correct: Plays a best response to others
items.why: It's Plays a best response to others. “Maximises joint payoff” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Year Nash published his equilibrium paper:
items.why: Exact answer: 1950. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
